coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for effective detection of copper ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: Investigating spintronic and advanced functional materials using synchrotron x-rays
coredata.dc:creator: Tam, Tran Van
Choi, Won Mook
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a green and simple one-pot route was developed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (a-GQDs) via hydrothermal process without any further modification or surface passivation. We synthesized the a-GQDs using glucose as the carbon source and ammonium as a functionalizing agent without the use of a strong acid, oxidant, or other toxic chemical reagent. The as-obtained a-GQDs have a uniform size of 3–4 nm, high contents of amino groups, and show a bright green emission with high quantum yield of 32.8%. Furthermore, the a-GQDs show effective fluorescence quenching for Cu2+ ions which can serve as effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+. The fluorescent probe using the obtained a-GQDs exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Cu2+ with the limit of detection as low as 5.6 nM. The mechanism of the Cu2+ induced fluorescence quenching of a-GQDs can be attributed to the electron transfer by the formation of metal complex between Cu2+ and the amino groups on the surface of a-GQDs. These results suggest great potential for the simple and green synthesis of functionalized GQDs and a practical sensing platform for Cu2+ detection in environmental and biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Copper ion
Sensor
Fluorescence

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: Investigating spintronic and advanced functional materials using synchrotron x-rays

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2018 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation for a-GQDs.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of a-GQDs and (c) their particle size distributions of a-GQDs.
(a) XPS survey scan of GQDs and a-GQDs. (b) High resolution XPS spectra of C1s for GQDs. High resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s and (d) N1s for a-GQDs.

(a) FT-IR spectra of GQDs and a-GQDs. UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of (b) GQDs and (c) a–GQDs. The inset shows the photograph images of GQDs and a-GQDs solutions under UV light (365 nm). (d) Fluorescence spectra of a–GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 380 nm to 500 nm.

(a) The change of fluorescence of a–GQDs solutions with different Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 20 μM. (b) The photographs of a-GQDs solutions in the absence (left) and presence (right) of Cu2+ (20 μM) under UV light (365 nm). (c) Schematic presentation of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of a-GQDs in the presence of Cu2+.

(a) The different fluorescence change (F/F0) of a–GQDs solution in the presence of various metal ions. (b) The fluorescence change (F/F0) of a-GQDs on the concentration of Cu2+ ions ranging from 0 to 300 μM. The inset shows the Stern-Volmer plots for the concentration of Cu2+ over the range of 0–100 nM.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots for effective detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44160, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a green and simple one-pot route was developed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (a-GQDs) via hydrothermal process without any further modification or surface passivation. We synthesized the a-GQDs using glucose as the carbon source and ammonium as a functionalizing agent without the use of a strong acid, oxidant, or other toxic chemical reagent. The as-obtained a-GQDs have a uniform size of 3–4 nm, high contents of amino groups, and show a bright green emission with high quantum yield of 32.8%. Furthermore, the a-GQDs show effective fluorescence quenching for Cu2+ ions which can serve as effective fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+. The fluorescent probe using the obtained a-GQDs exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity toward Cu2+ with the limit of detection as low as 5.6 nM. The mechanism of the Cu2+ induced fluorescence quenching of a-GQDs can be attributed to the electron transfer by the formation of metal complex between Cu2+ and the amino groups on the surface of a-GQDs. These results suggest great potential for the simple and green synthesis of functionalized GQDs and a practical sensing platform for Cu2+ detection in environmental and biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, considerable effort has been made to synthesize a new class of materials of quantum dots (QDs) to substitute traditional semiconductor QDs due to their high cost and toxicity in human health and the environment. In this regard, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the zero-dimensional form of graphene with lateral dimensions smaller than 20 nm, has attracted considerable interest in both theoretical and experimental research owing to their excellent optical and electrical characteristics and superior biocompatibility [1,2]. Unlike traditional semiconductor QDs, GQDs have excellent properties including low cytotoxicity, ease of synthesis, high stability and stable luminescence against photo bleaching [3–6]. Thus, the GQDs show remarkable prospects in various applications such as cellular imaging, optoelectronic devices, and fluorescent probes [7–9]. Currently, various top-down and bottom-up methods are being investigated and reported to develop the facile preparation of GQDs. For the typical top-down preparation method, large graphene oxide sheets are cut into nm-sized pieces of graphene using chemical oxidation or hydrothermal process [10–12]. However, non-uniform morphology, low production yields and low quantum yields of GQDs limit their mass production. In contrast, bottom-up methods produce GQDs by carbonizing small hydrocarbon molecules under catalytic or thermal reactions, which provides a simple and green approach for the production of high quality GQDs with large-scale [13,14].

Copper ions (Cu2+) play a critical role in environmental and biological systems, and are combined with many proteins and enzymes for achieving important metabolic processes such as bone growth, connective tissue and neutralization of free radicals [15,16]. Due to the non-biodegradability, Cu2+ can accumulate in human and animal livers through bioaccumulation. The over-accumulation of Cu2+ in the human body causes serious diseases, including genetic disorders such as Wilson's disease and Alzheimer's diseases [17]. According the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the limit level of Cu2+ in drinking water is set at 1.3 ppm (20 μM) [18]. Thus, considerable demand has arisen for a simple, green, highly reliable and sensitive detection method to qualify and quantify the concentration of Cu2+ in the environment and its ecosystems. Various analytical techniques have been developed for quantitatively detecting Cu2+, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, electrochemical methods, and atomic absorption spectrometry [19]. Although these techniques may detect the level of Cu2+ with high sensitivity, they require relatively high cost apparatus as well as complicated and time consuming procedures; they are therefore not suitable for in-field applications.

In recent years, the fluorescence sensing technique for metal ions has attracted considerable attention because of its excellent sensitivity, selectivity, ease of operation, and ability to detect with a non-destructive manner for real-time monitoring [20]. The fluorescent nanomaterials including semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoparticles have been used for the fluorescence sensing materials in the detection of metal ions owing to their high quantum yield and size-tunable emission profiles [21,22]. However, these nanomaterials are limited in their sensor potential due to their toxicity and expensive synthesis method. Recently, the use of fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the detection of metal ions has been reported in various studies as an alternative to semiconductor quantum dots due to their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good solubility in polar and non-polar solvents [2]. Despite the aforementioned production methods, promising methods still need to be developed for the synthesis of GQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield as well as excellent selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of metal ions.

Herein, we have demonstrated a facile and green one-pot synthesis of highly fluorescent amino-functionalized GQDs (a-GQDs) through the hydrothermal process without any further modification or surface passivation. In this work, the a-GQDs are synthesized through the carbonization of glucose in the presence of ammonia and hydrogen peroxide without the use of a strong acid, oxidant, or other toxic chemical reagent, implying that our synthesis technique is a relatively simple and environmental friendly method. The obtained a-GQDs possess a uniform size distribution of 3–4 nm with a few layers of graphene, and the primary amine groups are successfully introduced onto the lattice of GQDs with high doping contents. Moreover, the a-GQDs show high quantum yield of 32.8% with a strong green emission by the successful introduction of amino functional groups into the GQDs. Based on these superior features, the a-GQDs are employed in a novel fluorescent probe for the specific detection of Cu2+ ions, which exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ detection with a low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The a-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of glucose (99%, Sigma-Aldrich) with ammonia (NH3, 30%, Sigma-Aldrich) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 25%, Sigma-Aldrich) through one-pot hydrothermal treatment. In brief, glucose aqueous solution (40 mL, 4 mg/mL) was mixed with ammonia (10 mL) and hydrogen peroxide (5 mL) and then stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 4 h. After the mixture solution naturally cooled down to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane. The resulting yellowish solution was then dialyzed using dialysis tubing (3000 Da, Spectrum Lab. Inc) against DI water for 1 day to remove impurities and unreacted hydrogen peroxide and ammonia, resulting in an aqueous a-GQDs solution. The concentration of as-synthesized a-GQDs aqueous solution is ca. 1.7 mg/mL with product yield of ca. 53%. For the synthesis of pristine GQDs, the same procedure was followed without the addition of ammonia and hydrogen peroxide.

The nanostructures and morphology of a-GQDs were observed with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher) measurements were taken using monochromatic AlKα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV), where XPS samples were prepared by drop-casting 1 mL a-GQDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) on a SiO2/Si substrate. UV–Visible absorption spectrum was obtained using Specord 210 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Analytik Jena), and Raman Spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, DXR Raman spectrometer) was measured from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 at room temperature using a 532 nm excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured to determine the functional groups using the Nicolet IR 200 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). Fluorescence spectroscopy (Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent) was performed at room temperature.

For the detection test of Cu2+, different concentrations of Cu2+ solutions were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of Cu(NO3)2 in distilled water and the concentration of a-GQDs aqueous solution was adjusted at 0.03 mg/mL. In a typical experiment, 1 mL of a-GQDs solution was transferred into 1 mL of each solution with different concentrations of Cu2+, followed by retention for 5 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were subsequently recorded with the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. For the selectivity experiment, the fluorescence spectra were obtained using various metal ion solutions (Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd3+, Mn2+, Li+, Cr2+, Ca2+, Fe3+) with the similar procedure as that of Cu2+ detection.

The various fabrication methods for the functionalization of GQDs were reported through multiple processes that involve the preparation of GQDs and the subsequent functionalization of GQDs. However, the synthesis method for the a-GQDs in this work was performed via simple one-pot hydrothermal process with glucose as a carbon precursor in the presence of ammonia as `depicted in Fig. 1 . In this method, glucose molecules react and convert into graphene framework [23–25], and ammonia with hydrogen peroxide may contribute to the reaction of functionalization with amino groups at the edges of the GQDs. The amino and other oxygen functionalities of a-GQDs provide a high solubility and stability in aqueous media with high quantum yield. The morphology of a-GQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM). Fig. 2 a shows a representative TEM image of a-GQDs dispersed in water. It can be observed that the obtained a-GQDs are mostly spherical particles with relatively uniform distribution, providing an average size of 4.34 nm (Fig. 2c). In the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2b, a relatively high crystalline structure is observed in the a-GQDs with obvious lattice fringes, consisting of an interplane spacing of 0.24 nm which responds to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [26,27]. These results indicate that the a-GQDs with high quality were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal process using glucose as the carbon precursor.

The chemical composition of a-GQDs was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the survey displays clear peaks at of 532, 400 and 285 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively, whereas the peaks of C1s and O1s are only observed in the survey scan of the pristine GQD. The atomic contents of the three elements in the a-GQDs are calculated to be 63.1% (C), 30.1% (O), and 6.8% (N), suggesting the high concentration of amino functionality in the a-GQDs using ammonia as the precursor. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of GQDs and a-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3b–c. It can be seen from the spectrum of a-GQDs in Fig. 3c that the CC bond at 284.4 eV, the CN bond at 285.7 eV, the carbon hydroxyl groups (COH) at 286.6 eV, and carboxylate carbon group (OCO) at 288.7 eV. Compared to the C1s spectrum of GQDs shown in Fig. 3b, the new peak of CN bond confirms the presence of N atoms from the amino group of the a-GQDs. The peak intensities of COH and OCO decrease for a-GQDs, which indicates that few oxygen functional groups are formed in the a-GQDs by the reaction of ammonia with oxygen groups to form a primary amine (NH2). To further study the bond configuration of N atoms in a-GQDs, the high-resolution N1s spectrum of a-GQDs is then collected as shown in Fig. 3d. The spectrum can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.3 and 401.2 eV, which can be assigned to the amine band and CN bonding, respectively. The XPS results support that the a-GQDs are successfully synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal process.

The surface chemistry of a-GQDs was evaluated by FT-IR. Fig. 4 a shows the FT-IR spectra of GQDs and a-GQDs. The FT-IR spectrum of the pristine GQDs exhibits broad absorption peaks of the H-bonded associated OH at around 3501 cm−1. The peaks at 1431, 1625 cm−1, and 1720 cm−1 correspond to the groups of the deformation of CO, in-plane vibration of sp
2 bonded carbon atoms and the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group, respectively. In comparison, it can be seen that two additional peaks relating to the amine functional group are found in the FT-IR spectrum of a-GQDs. The two characteristic peaks appear at 3146 and 1352 cm−1, which are ascribed to the NH bending vibration and the CN bending vibration, respectively. These new peaks obviously indicate the successful formation of amino functional group on the GQDs surface. Fig. 4b and c shows the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of GQDs and a-GQDs, respectively. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of a-GQDs exhibit an observable two absorption peak located at about 322 nm and 442 nm, which are attributed to the electron transition from π→π* of aromatic sp
2 domain and n→π of the amino group at the edges of a-GQDs, respectively, while the π→π* transition peak of aromatic sp
2 domain at 275 nm is only found for the GQDs. Also, it was found in the fluorescent spectra of GQDs and a-GQDs that the maximum fluorescent peak position of a-GQDs is at ca. 500 nm, which is red-shifted from ca. 420 nm of GQDs. The inset images of Fig. 4b and c shows photographs of GQDs and a-GQDs aqueous solutions taken under 365 nm UV light, respectively. It is clearly shown that the a-GQDs exhibit a green fluorescence color, while the pristine GQDs exhibit a blue fluorescence color. The functionalization of GQDs with amino group is attributed to this red-shift behavior. As reported previously, the functional groups covalently bonded on the GQDs could lead to the changes of the electronic structure of GQDs. The electron donation can occurred from the unpaired electron of amino functional groups to the GQDs, causing the electron density increase of a-GQDs [28,29], which lowers the band gap of a-GQDs and results in a red shift in the fluorescent spectra. The calculated quantum yield (Q) of a-GQDs based on the fluorescence (Table S1) is determined to be about 32.8%. This value is much higher than that of the pristine GQDs (4.5%). The enhanced quantum yield of the a-GQDs could be attributed to the decrease of oxygen groups by the amino group functionalization, such as the hydroxyl, carboxylic and epoxy groups which are known as the non-radiative electro-hole recombination centers. In addition, the fluorescent spectra of a-GQDs are explored with different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 4d. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 380 to 500 nm, the maximum fluorescent peaks of a-GQDs obviously vary in a wide range from 500 nm to 560 nm by changing the excitation wavelength. It is noticed that the highest fluorescent intensity of a-GQDs is recorded by the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. As excited by longer wavelength from 460 to 500 nm, the fluorescent intensity of a-GQDs decreased with red-shifted peak. These results indicate that the a-GQDs show an excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior, which is related to the surface states of different energy levels due to the various functional groups on the surface of a-GQDs.

We explored the fluorescence response of a-GQDs toward Cu2+ for the feasibility of Cu2+ detection. Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence intensity change (F/F
0 ) of the a-GQDs solution in the presence and absence of Cu2+ with the excitation wavelength of 440 nm. The fluorescence intensity of a-GQDs at around 500 nm remarkably decreased with the addition of Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of a-GQDs. When the concentration of Cu2+ increased from 100 nM to 20 μM, the obvious and gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity of a-GQDs is observed. This indicates that the detection system using a-GQDs is very sensitive to the Cu2+ concentration. In addition, Fig. 5b shows the photographs of a-GQDs solution under the 365 nm UV light in the absence and presence of Cu2+. It can be clearly observed that the green fluorescence of a-GQDs solution was quenched upon the addition of Cu2+. This fluorescence quenching might be ascribed to the electron transfer from the excited state of a-GQDs to Cu2+ as described in Fig. 5c. Since Cu2+ could form a metal complex through the strong electrostatic attraction and rapid chelation with the amino groups on the surface of a-GQDs, Cu2+ as an electron acceptor could facilitate the photo-induced electron transfer from a-GQDs [30,31].

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed sensing system, the fluorescent change of a-GQDs solution was examined with the various biologically and environmentally relevant metal ions, including Hg2+, Ca2+, Li+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cd3+, Mn2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions (20 μM). As shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S1, the substantial fluorescence quenching of the a-GQDs solution is observed with the addition of Cu2+, while the slight and negligible quenching effect is shown with the addition of other metal ions. Fig. 6b exhibits the relationship of the fluorescence quenching of a-GQDs toward the concentration of Cu2+. A good linear response range of the fluorescence quenching is observed from 10 nM to 100 nM, which can be used to quantitatively determine the concentration of Cu2+. In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to analyze this linear relation as shown in the inset of Fig. 6b.
(
F 0
F
)
= 1 +
K
s v
[ C ]
Here F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of a-GQDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ ions, respectively. K
sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [C] is the concentration of Cu2+. The calculated K
sv is 7.069 × 10−4 by fitting the plot into the Stern-Volmer equation and the linear plot was observed with a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9971. Based on these results, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 5.6 nM according to the 3σ IUPAC criteria, which is a lower value than the limit content of ions in drinking water established by the U.S. EPA. These results show that the Cu2+ detection using a-GQDs exhibits superior sensitivity and might be very useful in environmental applications. These results clearly indicate that the fluorescent sensor using a-GQDs has high selectivity for Cu2+ ions over other metal ions, which is attributed to the high recognition of amino groups on the surface of a-GQDs toward Cu2+.

This report shows one-pot facile synthesis of highly fluorescent a-GQDs from the carbonization of glucose through hydrothermal treatment in the presence of ammonia. The synthesized a-GQDs aqueous solution showed a bright green luminescence and high quantum yield of 32.8% with a uniform size of about 4.34 nm. Furthermore, the prepared a-GQDs were used as highly efficient and sensitive fluorescence sensors for the detection of Cu2+ with the low detection limit of 5.6 nM. The mechanism of the fluorescence sensor using a-GQDs can be attributed to the efficient formation of the complex between Cu2+ and amino groups on the surface of a-GQDs, resulting in the electron transfer from the excited state of a-GQDs to Cu2+. Our study offers a facile and green approach to the synthesis of functionalized GQDs, while demonstrating a simple, sensitive, selective and low-cost sensing system for Cu2+. The prepared a-GQDs can also be used in various novel applications, including biological applications and optoelectronics, due to their tunable optical properties, low cytotoxicity, and excellent solubility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the 2017 Research Fund of the University of Ulsan.
